coredata.dc:description: Phase change materials (PCMs) enable efficient transient thermal management due to their high latent heat of fusion. However, the relatively low PCM thermal conductivity limits heat transfer and power density of PCM-based approaches. Another key challenge is the buildup of a thick melt layer, which reduces heat transfer over time. Dynamic PCMs (dynPCMs) address the melt layer challenge by pumping away the liquid PCM as it melts to maintain a thin melt layer. This paper investigates the heat transfer and flow physics of dynPCM cooling on high thermal conductivity copper extended surface heat sinks. Extensive thermal and optical measurements coupled with high fidelity 3D numerical modeling are used to evaluate the performance of dynPCM on finned surfaces and understand the underlying heat transfer and flow physics. The study explores a range of operating conditions, applied power (35–70 W) and added mass (0–1.5 kg), for three copper heat sinks, two finned and one flat. Measurement results show that dynPCM on the finned sinks lower the thermal resistance by up to 45 % when compared to the flat sample. At 70 W applied power and any added mass, the finned sinks have an average of 4.4 °C lower steady state temperature when compared to the flat heat sink. The 3D modeling shows that the highest heat fluxes occur at the tips of the fins near the center of the heat sink where the melt layer is thinnest and away from where the liquid PCM drains out. This work details the effect of extended surfaces on dynPCM thermal management and shows the potential for further performance enhancement of dynPCM approaches based on gained fundamental understanding of governing mechanisms.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phase change materials (PCMs) enable efficient transient thermal management due to their high latent heat of fusion. However, the relatively low PCM thermal conductivity limits heat transfer and power density of PCM-based approaches. Another key challenge is the buildup of a thick melt layer, which reduces heat transfer over time. Dynamic PCMs (dynPCMs) address the melt layer challenge by pumping away the liquid PCM as it melts to maintain a thin melt layer. This paper investigates the heat transfer and flow physics of dynPCM cooling on high thermal conductivity copper extended surface heat sinks. Extensive thermal and optical measurements coupled with high fidelity 3D numerical modeling are used to evaluate the performance of dynPCM on finned surfaces and understand the underlying heat transfer and flow physics. The study explores a range of operating conditions, applied power (35–70 W) and added mass (0–1.5 kg), for three copper heat sinks, two finned and one flat. Measurement results show that dynPCM on the finned sinks lower the thermal resistance by up to 45 % when compared to the flat sample. At 70 W applied power and any added mass, the finned sinks have an average of 4.4 °C lower steady state temperature when compared to the flat heat sink. The 3D modeling shows that the highest heat fluxes occur at the tips of the fins near the center of the heat sink where the melt layer is thinnest and away from where the liquid PCM drains out. This work details the effect of extended surfaces on dynPCM thermal management and shows the potential for further performance enhancement of dynPCM approaches based on gained fundamental understanding of governing mechanisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid adoption of high-power electrification technologies with transient heat loads creates an increasingly critical need for effective thermal management solutions [1–3]. Failure to maintain temperatures within the recommended operating range leads to reductions in performance and lifetime for electronic components [4]. Conventional thermal management solutions such as air cooling and single-phase liquid cooling must be designed to handle peak loads which often leads to oversized systems with inefficient operation [5]. Phase change materials (PCMs) present a promising approach for high-power, transient thermal management due to their high latent heat of fusion which enables the system to absorb a large quantity of heat during the phase transition [6–10]. This ability to absorb highly transient heat loads enables efficient thermal management architectures which can be designed and sized for the base heat load rather than the peak heat load.

Phase change material heat transfer is primarily defined by three properties: density, ρ, latent heat, L, and the thermal conductivity, k. A proposed figure of merit, FOM = ρLk, combines the three properties, with a higher value indicating a higher PCM heat absorption effectiveness [11,12]. As many common organic PCMs have low intrinsic thermal conductivities, the achievable power density will likewise be low. Many prior studies focus on improving the thermal conductivity of a PCM-based approach by incorporating high thermal conductivity materials together with organic PCMs. Such work includes composite materials with metallic foams [13–15] or highly conductive nanoparticles [16–19]. Additional studies create PCM infused heat sinks by embedding high thermal conductivity heat sinks with PCMs [20–22]. While these strategies have shown the ability to increase the effective thermal conductivity and power density of the thermal management system, another fundamental limit of PCMs exists: in any stationary PCM configuration, the melting is governed by the Stefan problem in which the melt front moves away from the heat source [23,24]. The melt front migration leads to increased thermal resistance over time, so the heat transfer starts high while the melt layer is thin but quickly diminishes as the melt layer grows.

Close contact melting (CCM) has been studied as a strategy to overcome the limitation presented by the melt front migration [25]. In typical CCM configuration the PCM is heated from the bottom and is not mechanically constrained, allowing gravity to act on the higher density solid PCM and thus moving it towards the heated surface, displacing the lower density liquid PCM and reducing the liquid layer thickness [26–28]. CCM can achieve higher power densities than conventional fixed PCM configurations and has been validated experimentally and numerically for a variety of PCM [29–31], container geometries [32–34], and boundary conditions [35–38]. However, CCM still exhibits a reduction in heat transfer over time. This is because as solid PCM melts, the gravitational force decreases and the liquid layer thickness increases. It is also naturally limited by the densities of the materials involved and by the distinctive driving force direction.

Recently, several studies have explored the concept of pressure-enhanced CCM or dynamic phase change materials (dynPCM) for both energy storage and thermal management applications [39–41]. In dynPCM, an external force applied to the solid PCM pumps away the liquid during melting to maintain a near constant, thin melt layer and corresponding low thermal resistance throughout the melting process. DynPCM achieves both high power and energy densities and shows the ability to maintain constant surface temperatures or heat fluxes for fixed applied heat fluxes or surface temperatures, respectively. Different methods for applying the external force have been studied but in all cases the force is greater than that of the gravitational force acting on the PCM itself, so there is little decay in the heat transfer performance over time [42–45]. Also, the external force, unlike gravity, may be applied in any desirable direction. Based on these initial findings, researchers have conducted studies on dynPCM performance with spatially heterogeneous heat inputs and pulsed heat inputs [46,47], integration of dynPCM with cold plates [48], and recharge of dynPCM systems [49]. However, there remains a need to explore the heat transfer and fluid flow of dynPCM on extended surfaces as a way for overcoming the low intrinsic thermal conductivity of organic PCMs. Only limited work has been done to understand PCM heat transfer on extended surfaces as a method to enhance CCM [50] and dynPCM performance [51].

A simple scaling analysis reveals that dynPCM thermal management techniques are enhanced by the addition of fins to the heat spreader. Since the intrinsic thermal conductivity of the PCM is low, the thermal resistance across the PCM melt layer (even with integration with force) remains the major thermal resistance when compared to the spreader thermal resistance or the conduction or convection resistance on the heat source. Appendix A provides analysis of dynPCM thermal resistances and the scaling analysis.

Since only a limited number of potentially applicable configurations can be studied experimentally, it is essential to use numerical simulation tools to explore the performance of various systems of interest, including PCM-based heat sinks [52]. One of the earliest works is the comprehensive computational analysis performed by Humphries and Griggs [53], who investigated melting in a PCM-based parallel plate fins heat sink, with uniform heat flux at the base and adiabatic conditions at all other boundaries. They examined the effects of geometric properties (e.g., fin height, thickness and spacing) and assumed conduction as the only heat transfer mechanism. More recently, Shatikian et al. [21,54] numerically explored plate-fin PCM-based sinks with a uniform temperature and uniform heat flux at the base. It was observed that the ratio of latent to total heat accumulated in the system increases as the fin height increases, which in turn expedites the melting process. Saha and Dutta [55] numerically investigated a plate fin heat sink filled with paraffin. They showed that for a tall system (higher fin height), heat is transferred to the PCM mainly from the fins, as the base interface with the PCM is mainly composed of the formed liquid layer with high thermal resistance. Additional numerical studies show that pin fins are more effective for transferring heat to the domain around them [56]. Akula & Balaji [57] investigated several configurations of fins, where they also observed reduced temperatures for pin fins. These findings were supported by the value of the liquid fraction, which was larger for the pin fin array for similar instances, indicating that a more rapid heat dissipation rate induced by natural convection accelerated the melting process. Several studies showed that increasing the number of fins, while keeping a constant volume ratio between the fins and PCM, resulted in an improvement of the heat sink’s performance due to a larger surface area between the fins and the PCM [58].

There exists a broad variety of numerical methods that are routinely applied in an analysis of PCM-based systems. There are cases in which significant simplifications are possible, especially when the modeling is done on a system or device level. These simplified models save significant computation time while still being able to capture the most important features of the device performance [59]. However, in many cases simplified modeling overlooks essential details of the process [60]. In common PCM systems, this is true when natural convection must be accounted for in melting [61], or when void creation is expected in solidification [62]. In CCM modeling, the details of processes within the thin molten layer are of utmost importance [30,31,37,38]. Dynamic melting over a finned surface is characterized by many subtle but highly important intrinsic features such as the melting process intensity at various locations. Thus, detailed numerical modeling is essential to explore the coupled heat transfer and fluid flow of dynPCM in the heat sinks explored.

This work uses transient thermal measurements, optical velocity measurements, and high-fidelity numerical modeling to investigate the heat transfer and fluid flow physics of dynPCM on high thermal conductivity extended surface heat sinks. The operating conditions including applied power, added mass, and the heat sink geometry are investigated to determine their impact on surface temperature, thermal resistance, melt velocity, and heat flux distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic phase change materials on extended surfaces

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the design of the finned sinks used. Fig. 2(a) shows the schematic of the cross section of two fins. The designs are rectangular strip fins with a fin width (w) of 1 mm, a spacing (s) of 2 mm between fins, and fin heights (h) of either 1 mm or 7 mm. The fins are arrayed on circular bases with a diameter of 42.2 mm and a thickness (th) of 3.5 mm. Fig. 2(b) shows a photograph of the heat sink with h = 1 mm tall fins, while Fig. 2(c) shows a photograph of the heat sink with h = 7 mm tall fins. In addition to the two finned heat sinks, a flat plate with the same base dimensions was used as a comparison case. Each sink has a thermocouple hole 1.5 mm below the base surface centered along the perimeter, and extending into the center of the sample (visible in Fig. 2b). All three heat sinks are made of C101 copper and were fabricated using wire electrical discharge machining. The tabs on the left and right sides of the heat sinks (shown in Figs. 2b, c) are for mounting samples to the dynPCM experimental fixture.

Fig. 3 shows the measurement setup used for evaluating the dynPCM thermal management systems. Fig. 3(a) shows a schematic of the setup and Fig. 3(b) shows a photograph of the setup. The applied pressure is provided using a weighted piston to push the PCM sample onto the copper surfaces. The PCM used is an inexpensive paraffin wax (CAS #8002–74–2, Thermo Fisher) with a melting temperature of 58 °C. The melting point of the selected paraffin is appropriate for common thermal management applications. Many electronic systems have operating requirements under 85 °C [63,64], so the PCM with a melting temperature difference of >25 °C is advantageous for providing adequate thermal management for a range of heat fluxes. Additionally, the paraffin selected is a commonly available PCM, non-toxic, thermochemically stable, and is reasonably representative of other organic PCMs [40,65]. Table 1 shows the full thermophysical properties of the paraffin used [66]. Consistent PCM samples are prepared by pouring fully liquid PCM into 3D printed cylindrical molds. Once samples fully cool (solidify), they are removed from the mold and pressed onto a warmed finned sink (hotter than the melting temperature of the PCM) to fill the gaps between fins with PCM. This is done to improve correspondence between the experiments and modeling, described in Section 4. The PCM embedded sink is cooled to room temperature before beginning a measurement. Electric heating pads (35475K163, McMaster Carr) provide the heat input to the system from below each sample. Three heating pads are connected in parallel to a 2000VA variable AC power supply (TDGC2–2KM Metered Variac, Automation Technologies). A digital power meter (P06S-100, Puucai) connected to the power supply enables the measurement and precise control over the input power for each measurement. The sinks are interchangeable and during the measurements are bolted down onto the heater with a thin layer of thermal paste (3883K104, McMaster Carr) in-between to ensure good thermal contact. A 3D printed stabilizer ensures that the piston with the added mass, sample, and sink remain axially aligned throughout the measurement process and a plate under the system collects the liquid PCM as it is pushed away from the heat source. A k-type thermocouple (5SC-TT-K-36–36, Omega) measures the temperature in the base of the sink throughout the melting. Prior to experiments, the thermocouple is calibrated to an accuracy of ± 0.2 ⁰C using a thermostatic bath. A camera (RZ19–02,320,100-R3U1, Razer) is positioned to capture the melting process at the interface between the copper surface and solid PCM. An additional camera is positioned to capture the motion of the piston as the wax melts to measure the sinking velocity.

The heating power is 35, 45, 60, or 70 W, which correspond to heat fluxes of 2.5, 3.2, 4.3 or 5.0 W/cm2. The heat flux is calculated using the cross-sectional area of the sink base, 14 cm2. The heating power and corresponding heat fluxes are selected in line with recent studies of PCM based thermal management [46,49,67,68]. The selected powers and heat fluxes reflect moderate power and heat flux applications such as LED arrays [69] and CPUs [70]. These powers and heat fluxes fall in the broad range of heat loads for electronic chip applications which can vary between 1 W to 1 kW heat dissipation, and up to 1 kW/cm2 heat flux [71–74]. Added masses of 500 g, 1000 g, and 1500 g are used in addition to a 0 g case corresponding to the self-weight of the PCM sample. In total, 30 dynPCM experiments are conducted and analyzed.

The numerical model was constructed in three dimensions to replicate the experimental setup while applying geometric scaling and symmetry to reduce computational cost. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the simulated domain includes only the central portion of the heat sink by taking advantage of the geometric symmetry of the system. Specifically, a longitudinal section, corresponding to one-half of the full cylindrical base, was modeled, such that the rear symmetry plane (yx) divides the domain vertically along the centerline of the cylindrical heat sink.

The modeling approach retains the essential flow and thermal characteristics while reducing the computational load by a factor of two. The experimental heat sink featured a circular base 42.2 mm in diameter, while the numerical domain spans a 21.5 mm × 12 mm rectangular region, corresponding to approximately one-half of the heat sink, with a symmetry boundary condition imposed at the rear (yx) plane. To further reduce computational cost, only the four central flow canals were modeled, see Fig. 4(b), laterally bounded by symmetry planes located at the mid-width of the first and fifth fins. The configuration effectively includes three full fins and two half-fins, capturing the essential fin–PCM interaction region.

Fig. 4(c) shows the boundary conditions: to represent the heater, a uniform heat flux was applied at the bottom surface (zx plane). The applied heat flux was computed by converting the experimental heating power into an equivalent surface heat flux based on the heater–PCM contact area. The top zx plane was defined as a pressure-inlet with zero gauge pressure, allowing free motion of the solid PCM through it, without any prescribed velocity or mass flow. The front yx boundary was treated as an outlet-vent, enabling melted PCM to exit the domain. Symmetry boundary conditions were applied to the lateral (two yz planes) and rear (yx) faces. The temperature of the solid PCM entering from the top was set to 26.85 ⁰C (300 K), reflecting the room temperature typical for the experiments.

where p is the static pressure, µ is the dynamic viscosity, ρg is the vertical gravitational body force per unit volume, Fy is the vertical external force per unit volume, u is the x-direction velocity, v is the vertical velocity in y-direction, and w is the z-direction velocity, α is the mushy zone parameter, β is the liquid fraction, defined based on the local temperature, and ε is a small constant (e.g., 0.001) used to prevent division by zero. Other equations (e.g. energy) and definitions used are common for the solid-liquid phase change modeling [21].

Reflecting the experimental conditions, the domain comprised two materials: copper for the solid heat sink (the base and fins in one piece) and n-Octacosane for the PCM, which is very close to the paraffin used in the experiments and possesses well-defined thermophysical properties. The relevant properties, including density, specific heat, thermal conductivity, and viscosity, were defined based on reliable literature sources [53,75–80]. The latent heat, solidus temperature and liquidus temperature were specified as constants. Table 2 shows the full thermophysical properties of n-Octacosane.

The fixed-grid mesh shown in Fig. 4(a) was fully structured with Cartesian cells of size 100 μm × 100 μm × 100 μm. To resolve thermal gradients near the fin–PCM interfaces during melting, five inflation layers of 15 μm minimum thickness were built locally. The final mesh comprised approximately 6 million cells, 18.7 million faces, and 6.25 million nodes distributed across 64 partitions. The model included two cell zones and 18 face zones. Appendix C contains detailed grid and time step independence study.

Phase change in the PCM was modeled using the enthalpy–porosity method which treats the phase-change area as a porous medium with a built-in momentum source term that suppresses velocity in the solid and mushy regions. The momentum source term depends nonlinearly on the liquid fraction, with its magnitude increasing as the cell transitions from liquid to solid, and is scaled by the mushy zone constant, which controls the rigidity of the solid phase. A constant mushy zone parameter of 1.0 × 107 kg/(m3·s) was used to ensure the solid PCM retained its structural form without unphysical motion. In addition to the built-in momentum source term mechanism, a separate momentum source term was introduced via user-defined functions (UDFs) to simulate the externally applied mechanical load. The force magnitude was scaled according to the simulated and experimental PCM bottom surface area ratio and was applied only to the solid phase in fully or partially solid PCM cells. The external force was modeled as a body force uniformly distributed across the solid phase, directly driving the downward motion of the solid PCM and accounting for the gravitational action of the applied weight in the experiment. The simulation was carried out in ANSYS Fluent using a pressure-based solver with a transient formulation. Laminar flow was assumed due to the low velocity regime.

The pressure–velocity coupling was handled using the SIMPLE algorithm, with PRESTO! for pressure discretization and QUICK schemes for momentum and energy equations. Gradient reconstruction employed the least-squares cell-based method. A bounded second-order implicit scheme was used for time integration. Under-relaxation factors were tuned to ensure numerical stability: 0.3 for pressure, 0.7 for momentum, 0.1 for liquid fraction, and 1.0 for energy. A fixed time step of 0.005 s was used to resolve the transient melting process with high temporal fidelity, as the refinement study showed practically no change for 5 ms results compared with 10 ms ones. The numerical model was carefully validated using the experimental results, as described in Sections 4 and 5.

Fig. 5 shows transient sample base temperature measurements for the three different heat sinks at input powers of 35 W and 70 W and added masses of 500 g and 1500 g. Fig. 5(a) shows the results for the heat sink with no fins, Fig. 5(b) shows the results for the sink with short (h = 1 mm) fins, and Fig. 5(c) shows the results for the sink with tall (h = 7 mm) fins. All measurements show the expected plateau region that occurs for dynPCM melting. The tall finned sink reaches the plateau slower than either the short finned sink or no-fin sink in all cases due to the larger thermal mass of the tall fins. For all three heat sinks, the plateau temperature varies with both the input power and added mass, with higher input power corresponding to higher plateau temperatures and higher added mass corresponding to lower plateau temperatures. Additionally, both finned sinks have lower plateau temperatures than the flat sink for all tested conditions. The experimentally observed steady-state plateaus seen in Fig. 5(c) agree well with the results from the numerical model, in which the bottom boundary temperature stabilizes at 5 to 6 °C above the melting temperature, see Fig. 6 (a) below.

Fig. 6 presents the modeled steady-state thermal field for a heating power of 35 W under an applied external load of 500 g. In Fig. 6(a), temperature profiles are extracted along the vertical direction of the fin array at both the front plane (facing the outlet, z = 21.5 mm) and the rear symmetry plane (z = 0 mm), which bounds the half-domain representation of the experimental setup. The temperature gradient within the 3.5 mm-thick copper base is strictly linear, indicating pure one-dimensional conduction. Above the base, the profiles exhibit pronounced curvature, signaling the transition to multi-dimensional heat transfer, along the fin and across it to the PCM. The melting temperature is indicated as a reference threshold. Fig. 6(b) shows the corresponding temperature contours. These contours highlight effective heat conduction from the base into the PCM, with intense thermal penetration along the fins. A significant thermal drop is observed just above the fin tips, emphasizing the enhancement effect of the fins. The agreement in sink temperature between the experiments and the predictions is very good, providing initial validation for the model.

Fig. 7 shows the calculated thermal resistance for the different surfaces at a variety of operating conditions. Thermal resistance is calculated as
R =
(
T
s s
−
T m
) / Q
where Q is the input power, Tss is the steady-state plateau temperature, and Tm is the melting temperature of the PCM. Fig. 7(a) shows the thermal resistance as a function of added mass with a fixed input power of 70 W. The top x-axis shows applied pressure calculated as
P = M g / A
where M is the added mass, g is the gravitational constant and A is the surface area of the top of the PCM column. Higher added mass corresponds to lower thermal resistances for all three surfaces, as the thickness of the liquid layer decreases with the additional applied force. The biggest jump occurs when going from the self-weight of the PCM column to 500 g added mass, which highlights the importance of the additional external force to pump away the liquid PCM in the dynPCM concept. Fig. 7(b) shows the thermal resistance as a function of the input power with a fixed added mass of 1000 g. The top x-axis shows heat flux as
q "
= Q /
A proj
. The secondary y-axis for both Figs. 7(a) and 7(b) shows a normalized thermal resistance calculated using the projected area (
A proj
) of the heat sinks. For all surfaces higher powers result in lower thermal resistances, which indicates that the temperature difference increases proportionally less than the heat input. For all powers in the tested range, the finned surfaces have lower thermal resistances than the flat surface. At higher powers, the difference in thermal resistance between the finned surfaces and the flat surface becomes more pronounced.

Fig. 8 shows experimental images of the melt layer around the finned sinks. Figs. 8(a, b) show representative images of the tall and short finned sinks respectively. The images are captured once the heat sink base temperature reaches its steady state. The melt front forms a parabolic-type shape around the fins with a thicker liquid layer at the base of the fin and a thinner liquid layer near the top of the fin. The thinnest liquid layer occurs at the horizontal tips of the fins. Fig. 8(c) shows zoomed-in images of the melt front around the front three fins for the sink with tall fins at 35 W and 70 W applied power and 500 g and 1000 g added mass. Increasing the applied power creates a thicker liquid layer along the entire length of the fin. Increasing the added mass decreases the liquid layer thickness around the fin. Fig. 8(d) shows zoomed-in images of the melt front around the front three fins for the sink with short fins at 35 W and 70 W applied power and 500 g and 1000 g added mass. The short fins show similar but less pronounced differences in melt layer thickness for increased applied powers and added masses when compared with the tall fins.

Fig. 9 compares the simulated velocity and phase distribution within the PCM domain with an experimental image of the melt front at 70 W heating and 500 g external load. In Fig. 9(a), velocity vectors are plotted on a vertical yz mid-plane between the fins, spanning from the rear symmetry plane (right) to the front outlet plane (left). The flow field is predominantly induced by melting: the space occupied by the liquid remains the same because the solid moves downwards, and this causes the liquid to flow out. This promotes efficient drainage of the molten PCM toward the outlet, resulting in a thinner melt layer. Figs. 9(b,d) present liquid fraction contours on the symmetry and front yx-planes, respectively. Fig. 9(c) provides the experimental visualization of the melting front under identical conditions. The experimental pattern, Fig. 9(c), clearly validates the numerical prediction at the same location, Fig. 9(d), confirming the pronounced melting between fins and the load-induced drainage effect. This contributes to a thinner liquid layer, reduced thermal resistance, and improved thermal performance of the dynPCM system.

Fig. 10 shows the details of the velocity measurements. A video is taken of the system during the entire melting process. The “Tracker Video Analysis and Modeling Tool” software [81] enables the analysis of the videos to extract both displacement and velocity as a function of time. The software has built-in functions to calibrate the coordinate system and scale of the objects in the video against a known dimension and track a selected target throughout the course of the video. The height of the axial stabilizer, 60 mm, is used as the known dimension for calibration. A line on the weighted piston serves as the tracking target for the software. The software matches the pixels of the tracking target between successive time steps, for the tracking of the piston velocity as time step of 0.33 s is used. Fig. 10(a) shows six still images taken from the video of the tall fins with 1000 g added mass and an input power of 35 W. The marked tracking target is used to evaluate the displacement over time. Once the melting begins, around 90 s, there is a linear descent of the tracking target until the melting is complete, and the piston rests on top of the fins. Fig. 10(b) shows the tracked displacement as a function of time starting at a height of 50 mm corresponding to the initial height of the PCM sample. The displacement represents the distance from the bottom of the weighted piston to the top plane of the copper sink base. The red dots represent the six times shown in the images from Fig. 10(a). The displacement curve remains flat initially until melting begins at which point the displacement starts to decrease linearly as the piston moves toward the copper sink and finally flattens out again once the piston reaches its resting position on top of the fins. The final displacement corresponds to the height of the fins. Fig. 10(c) shows the velocity of the piston as a function of time during the dynPCM melting process. A positive velocity indicates that the piston is getting closer to the surface. Again, the red dots represent the six instances shown in the images from Fig. 10(a). The velocity stays at 0 mm/s until the onset of melting, at which point the velocity increases to a near steady value and remains there for the duration of melting.

Fig. 11 shows the measured PCM velocities for the three sinks with different operating conditions. Fig. 11(a) shows the PCM velocity as a function of added mass with an applied power of 70 W. The top x-axis shows applied pressure calculated as
P = M g / A
where M is the added mass, g is the gravitational constant and A is the surface area of the top of the PCM column. For all added masses the PCM velocity for the finned sinks is higher than for the flat sink, which indicates that there is a higher rate of heat transfer into the PCM for the finned surfaces. There is an increase of at least 7.5 % in the PCM velocity between the measurements using only the PCM self-weight and measurements with any added mass. The differences between 500 g, 1000 g, and 1500 g added mass are <3 % and do not follow a consistent pattern across the different sinks. These results show that in the range of added masses tested there is a minimal effect on the PCM velocity. Any variation in the PCM velocity between the different surfaces and added masses is due to heat losses and indicates that the heat loss pathway is different for different samples. Fig. 11(b) shows the PCM velocity as a function of applied power for a fixed added mass of 1000 g. The top x-axis shows heat flux as
q "
=
Q /
A proj
. The theoretical velocity line represents the PCM velocity in the case of no losses in the system such that all power goes to melting the PCM. Higher applied powers results in higher PCM velocities for all three sinks with a linear increasing trend. At 45 W, 60 W, and 70 W applied power the finned sinks have higher PCM velocities than the flat sink while at 35 W the velocity does not vary significantly between the finned sinks and the flat sink.

Fig. 12 (a) shows simulated surface heat flux distribution on the finned sink for two heating powers (35 W and 70 W) and three external weights (500 g, 1000 g, 1500 g), illustrating the three-dimensional variations in local heat transfer to the PCM. Higher power and weight combinations result in more confined and intensified heat flux regions near the fin tips, driven by melt layer thinning and enhanced thermal coupling. Fig. 12(b) shows surface heat flux profiles extracted along the vertical direction of the fins, from the fin base to the fin tip, for both the front (outlet) and symmetry planes. The data confirm elevated heat flux near the fin tips, where melting is very intensive, followed by a gradual decay toward the base. While the heating power clearly influences the magnitude of the heat flux, the effect of external load on the heat flux appears to be secondary, as evidenced by the close overlap of the three curves for each power setting. The difference between the front and symmetry planes becomes more pronounced at higher heating powers, reflecting the role of liquid PCM drainage at the outlet.

The study investigates the heat transfer physics of dynPCM on extended surfaces. Most prior dynPCM research focused on dynPCM on flat surfaces [39–41,44,46,48,49]. The present study shows that dynPCM on high thermal conductivity extended surfaces can overcome the two fundamental limitations of PCMs for thermal management: melt front migration and growing thermal resistance layer (as the PCM melts) and low system thermal conductivity (as organic PCMs have low intrinsic thermal conductivities). In dynPCM, applying the external force on the PCM pumps away the melted PCM and maintains a thin, constant thickness melt layer between the phase change interface and the heat source. For thermal management applications, the constant thin melt layer means heat is removed faster than in conventional PCM configurations, which allows for lower surface temperatures and less temperature variation. Additionally, dynPCM is ideal for transient heat loads. Integrating dynPCM into a larger thermal management system enables the downsizing of other components such that the surrounding system is designed to handle the base head load while the dynPCM handles transient heat loads. By incorporating high thermal conductivity extended surfaces, the overall system effective thermal conductivity is raised, reducing the thermal resistance between the heat source and the phase change interface and increasing the rate of melting.

The study explores the effects of applied power, added mass, and fin height on surface temperature, thermal resistance, PCM velocity, heat flux distribution, and melt layer geometry using transient thermal and optical measurements in combination with high fidelity numerical models. The study shows that dynPCM on extended surfaces can reduce the system thermal resistance compared to dynPCM on a flat surface, while still maintaining a near constant surface temperature throughout the duration of melting. The introduction of high thermal conductivity extended surfaces also increases the heat transfer into the PCM, as reflected by the increase in PCM velocities for the finned sinks.

The results from the numerical simulations show most of the heat transfer takes place at the tips of the fins and higher heat fluxes are archived near the center of the system, away from where the liquid drains out. The images of the melting show that the melt layer forms a “parabolic” geometry around the fins which corresponds the heat flux distribution from the simulations. The highest heat flux occurs at the fin tip where the melt layer is thinnest, and the lowest heat flux occurs at the fin base where the melt layer is thickest. Such heat flux distributions support the result that there is no significant difference between the performance for the tall and short finned sinks.

The measurement results and modeling heat flux distribution also suggest that alternative fin designs could provide further improvement to the heat transfer performance with dynPCM on extended surface heat sinks. Non-rectangular fins could enable more uniform heat transfer on larger portions of the surface area to fully capture the enhancement of the extended surfaces. Such work will require detailed analysis of the melt layer that forms around the fins and how different operating conditions and fin geometries change the melt layer.

Further work is needed to integrate dynPCM at a system level. There remains a need to explore higher ranges of heat fluxes for applications such as high performance computing and electric vehicles which can have heat fluxes on the magnitude of 100 W/cm2 and are projected to exceed 1000 W/cm2 in the coming years [73,82]. For practical applications, a rechargeable dynPCM system will be needed that can provide long term thermal management in a compact form factor and provide excellent energy and power density of both the discharge and recharge operation. Such a system will need to be capable of containing or recirculating the melted PCM and rapid solidification to enable near continuous operation of dynPCM cooling. The development of a rechargeable dynCPM system and the integration at a system level provide promising opportunities for future study.

This paper reports the thermal performance and heat transfer physics of dynPCM on high thermal conductivity extended surface heat sinks. Through careful thermal and optical measurements and high-fidelity numerical simulations for a range of operating conditions, we show that dynPCM on extended surfaces sinks outperforms dynPCM on flat surfaces. The measurement results show that dynPCM on extended surface sinks have up to a 45 % reduction in thermal resistance and a 5.6 °C reduction in steady state surface temperature compared with dynPCM on flat surfaces. The numerical simulations show that for the explored fin geometries, most of the heat transfer takes place at the tip of the fin rather than along the sides. The heat flux distribution from the numerical simulations correlates with the inverse of the melt layer thicknesses around the fin that are observed during the measurements. DynPCM on extended surface heat sinks show promise for further improvement in overcoming the fundamental limitations of PCMs for thermal management and energy storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Appendix A. Analysis of DynPCM Thermal Resistance
ce:section.ce:para:
Dynamic Phase Change Material thermal management can still be limited by the challenges that face other PCM-based solutions, notably the low intrinsic thermal conductivity of common PCMs. Even with thin melt layers the thermal resistance of the liquid PCM can dominate the overall thermal resistance between the heat source and the phase change interface. Fig. A1 shows a thermal resistance model for 1D conduction between the heat source and the phase change interface. In the model the resistances RTIM, RHS , and Rmelt correspond to the thermal resistances of the thermal interface material (TIM), heat spreader/heated surface, the liquid PCM melt layer respectively.
Fig. A1
Schematic of a dynPCM system with the corresponding thermal resistance network between the heat source and the phase change interface.
Fig. A1

The thermal resistance is calculated as
R = t h / k A
for thickness th, thermal conductivity k, and area A. In the analysis we assume 1D conduction for dynPCM melting on a flat 20 mm2 circular plate. The TIM is a zinc oxide thermal paste (3883K104, McMaster Carr) and has a thermal conductivity of 5 W/(m·K). We assume a conservative estimate of the TIM thickness of 500 µm. The heat spreader is copper with a thermal conductivity of 398 W/(m·K) and a thickness of 3.5 mm. The liquid PCM has a thermal conductivity of 0.21 W/(m·K) and the thickness, δ, that depends on the operating conditions as described by equations from prior dynPCM literature [39]:

The thermophysical properties of the PCM are listed in Table 1. Fig. A2 shows ε plotted as a function of the input heat flux between 1 – 20 W/cm2 for three different applied pressures: P = 0.1, 1, and 10 kPa. The results show that the thermal resistance of the melt layer is larger than the heat spreader and TIM thermal resistances combined even with externally applied pressures. At higher heat fluxes the ratio becomes higher due to the increasing thickness of the lower thermal conductivity melt layer. The results present an opportunity for the incorporation of high thermal conductivity extended surfaces to raise the overall system thermal conductivity and reduce the thermal resistance between the heat source and the phase change interface.

We conducted an uncertainty analysis for all measured and calculated values. The uncertainties were calculated using uncertainty propagation based on temperature and power measurements. Thermocouples were calibrated using a thermal bath equipped with a resistance temperature detector (RTD) temperature sensor (5615, Fluke) with a temperature measurement uncertainty of ±0.012 °C. As the thermocouple was calibrated, the measurement uncertainty is calculated based on the calibration uncertainty and the repeatability of the thermocouple:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix C
ce:section.ce:section-title: Grid and Time Step Independence Study
ce:section.ce:para:
A grid and time-step independence study was conducted to ensure numerical accuracy under the following simulation conditions: P = 35 W, and external load M = 1500 g. Fig. C1 below presents close-up views of the computational mesh employed in the simulation domain for the three different grid resolutions examined in this study: 100 µm × 100 µm × 100 µm, 200 µm × 200 µm × 200 µm, and 300 µm × 300 µm × 300 µm. These configurations were selected to evaluate grid independence and maintain a balance between numerical accuracy and computational cost. To accurately capture the thermal and velocity gradients near the solid-liquid interface, especially around the fin surfaces where heat transfer is dominant, inflation layers were applied at the PCM to fin interface. This local refinement enhances resolution near the melting front without excessively increasing the total cell count. It is essential that each grid of the three variants listed above has a different arrangement of the inflation layers, with the latter being more refined for the generally more refined grid. The inflation layers near the fin-PCM interface play a crucial role in achieving convergence by accurately capturing the temperature gradients, even when larger cells are used in regions where heat transfer is less dominant.
Fig. C1
To-scale close-up views of the computational mesh for the three tested grid configurations: (a) 100 µm × 100 µm × 100 µm; (b) 200 µm × 200 µm × 200 µm; (c) 300 µm × 300 µm × 300 µm. Near-wall layers change accordingly with the overall grid refinement.
Fig. C1

The corresponding mesh statistics, including the number of elements and nodes for each case, are summarized in Table C1 below.
Table C1
Grid information for spatial resolution analysis.
Table C1

1 100 × 100 × 100 µm3

Fig. C2 shows the time evolution of ΔT, the bottom surface temperature above the melting point, for different mesh and time step configurations. In Figure 2(a), the grid resolution is fixed at 100 µm × 100 µm × 100 µm while varying the time step sizes: 5 ms, 10 ms, and 20 ms. The results indicate good agreement between the cases with Δt = 5 ms and 10 ms, while the simulation with Δt = 20 ms fails to converge and shows unstable behavior during the transient phase. In Fig. C2(b), the time step is fixed at Δt = 5 ms, and the element size is varied (100, 200, and 300 µm). The temperature curves converge well across all three mesh resolutions. These results confirm that a time step of 5 ms and a grid size of 100 µm × 100 µm × 100 µm (with accordingly built inflation layers) are appropriate and reliable choices for the simulations.
Fig. C2
